turns.text: Hello Genie, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're going through the birth experience, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s strange to be on this side of the desk. Usually, I’m the one charting the patient, not the one being charted. But yeah, I’m happy to talk, if it helps anyone else avoid feeling like I do right now.

To be honest, it’s been… heavy. Every time I walk into that clinic in Andover, I’m not just a person; I’m a "high-risk" box to be checked. My ankles are swollen, my head is constantly pulsing, and I know exactly what those blood pressure numbers mean before the nurse even finishes typing them into the system. Knowing the physiology doesn't make it any less terrifying. It actually makes it worse. I see the pity in their eyes when they realize I’m navigating this third pregnancy mostly on my own, balancing my kids and the bills, and suddenly I’m just "that complicated case" to them.

I try to be a "good patient"—I nod, I ask the right questions—but inside, I’m just waiting for the other shoe to drop. My biggest fear isn't the pain; it’s the thought of losing control when I’m on that table. I’m scared, plain and simple. I just want to make sure I’m still here for my other two kids when all this is over. That’s the only goal.

Before labour, what did you expect creating and using a birth plan to be like — how it would be received by staff and how much it would guide your care? And what actually happened with your birth plan during labour?

Honestly? I thought a birth plan was my chance to regain some agency. Because I’ve worked in health settings, I know how easily a patient’s voice gets drowned out by the beeping of monitors and the rush of a shift change. I spent hours writing mine out, trying to be reasonable—I didn’t ask for the moon, just for a bit of dignity and clear communication while managing the pre-eclampsia. I figured if I wrote it down, it would act like a shield, something the nurses and doctors would respect as a baseline for my care.

But when things started getting real, that piece of paper felt like a joke. Once the magnesium drip started and the alarms began going off, the "plan" completely evaporated. The staff was focused on the numbers—my blood pressure, the baby’s heart rate—which I get, I do. But they stopped seeing me. They didn't consult the paper; they just moved into emergency mode. I kept trying to voice my preferences, but they just looked at me with that "we know best" expression. It was like I wasn't even in the room for my own birth. I wasn't a mother having a baby anymore; I was just a clinical problem to be managed until the delivery was over.

Before labour, what did you expect your pain management options to be — the range available, how they'd be offered, and how you'd be supported in deciding? And what was the reality of pain management during your labour?

I went in thinking I’d be treated like a colleague, someone who could have a real conversation about the risks and benefits of different options. Because of my background, I understood the pharmacology, and I wanted to be part of the decision-making process. I assumed they’d explain the trade-offs—especially with the pre-eclampsia—and we’d decide together on what would keep me stable but still allow me to be present for the birth. I wanted to feel like a partner, not a subject.

But the reality was completely different. When the pain became intense, there was no conversation. It felt like they just defaulted to the "standard protocol" for high-risk patients without asking me what I felt I needed or what I was comfortable with. They acted like my input wasn't relevant because I was already a "complicated case." I felt pushed toward interventions I hadn't fully consented to, and my questions were met with quick, dismissive answers that made me feel like I was being a nuisance. It wasn't about my comfort or my choices anymore; it was about efficiency and mitigating their liability. I felt completely stripped of my autonomy, just a body they were trying to stabilize while everything else fell away.
Were there pain management options you wanted but weren't able to access? What got in the way?

I really wanted to avoid a complete epidural if I could, or at least delay it. I wanted to try moving around, to use different positions—anything to feel like I wasn't just strapped to a bed like a mannequin. I knew I had to be monitored closely because of the hypertension, but I kept asking if there was a middle ground, maybe just something to take the sharpest edges off the pain so I could breathe and stay focused.

But it was like talking to a brick wall. Every time I brought up wanting a different approach, the focus immediately snapped back to my blood pressure numbers. They made it sound like any deviation from their strict "high-risk" protocol was reckless. I felt like the "doctor" in me knew there were other ways to manage things, but the "patient" in me was being silenced by the fear they were projecting. They didn't see a woman trying to participate in her own birth; they saw a risk to be managed. The magnesium drip already made me feel foggy and trapped, and when I couldn't get them to hear my requests for alternatives, I just gave up. It’s hard to advocate for your own care when you’re already exhausted and terrified you’re going to die on the table.

When you arrived at the birth setting — whether hospital, birth centre, or home — what did you expect it to feel like in terms of safety, comfort, and how you'd be welcomed? And what was your actual experience of the environment when you arrived?

I expected it to feel like a transition—a place where I’d be taken under someone’s wing. Since I knew the risks, I went in with my guard up, but I secretly hoped the staff would see past the chart. I wanted a sense of security, you know? Like, "We’ve got you, Genie, you’re safe here." I expected a little bit of warmth, or at least a recognition that this was a huge, scary life event for me, not just another shift in the labor and delivery unit.

The reality was clinical and cold. The moment I walked through those double doors, the atmosphere was sterile and hurried. It felt like I was being processed by a machine. Nobody greeted me; they just looked at my file, saw the "pre-eclampsia" tag, and I felt the energy in the room shift. It wasn't welcoming; it was heavy with tension. I was immediately moved into a room filled with blinking monitors and harsh fluorescent lights. Everything screamed "emergency." There was no attempt to make the space feel human—no acknowledgment of me as a person, just a focus on the machines and the data. I felt like a liability waiting to happen the second I stepped inside. It made me want to bolt, but I knew I didn't have anywhere else to go.
What was the biggest surprise about the physical environment or the immediate atmosphere when you arrived?

The biggest surprise—and honestly, the most jarring part—was how quickly I was erased from the room. I’ve worked in hospitals for years, so I know the routine, but being on the other side of the bed is a total perspective shift. I expected that even in a high-risk unit, there would be a moment of humanity. I expected someone to look me in the eye and say, "Hi, I’m your nurse, we're going to take good care of you."

Instead, it felt like the technology had more presence than I did. The machines were the main characters. The nurses and doctors talked *over* me, adjusting settings and checking cables, while I was basically just an obstacle in the way of the equipment. The physical space felt so hostile—all hard surfaces, cold lighting, and that constant, rhythmic beeping that, in my state, sounded like a countdown clock. 

I think the most surprising part was how lonely it felt in a room full of people. I have two kids at home and I was so worried about them, and the coldness of that room just amplified that terror. It wasn't just a place to give birth; it was a place where my agency was slowly dismantled, piece by piece, until I was just a vessel for the monitors.

If your birth involved an unexpected intervention or complication, what did you expect the process of being informed and involved in those decisions to look like? And how were those decisions actually made and communicated in the moment?

I knew going in that with pre-eclampsia, things could pivot to an emergency in a heartbeat. I expected that if we needed to intervene—say, an induction or an assisted delivery—the doctor would pause for a second. I expected them to look at me, explain the *why* in plain language, and ask me what I thought. I’m a professional, for heaven’s sake; I can process data. I expected a collaborative conversation, even if it was fast. 

But when the blood pressure spiked and the team decided to force the delivery, that partnership never happened. It felt like a landslide. Someone shouted an order, someone else started pushing meds, and suddenly there were three more people in the room that I hadn't even met. Nobody asked me; they just announced what was happening *to* me. It was like I was a car being serviced in a pit crew—hands everywhere, things being changed and adjusted—without a single word being directed at me as a human being. 

They weren't "informing" me; they were narrating their own actions while I was already underwater. I felt like a passenger in my own body, watching the ceiling tiles go by, completely sidelined in the most important moment of my life. It wasn't a choice; it was an execution of protocol.
Did you feel you had genuine choice and sufficient information in those moments, or did decisions feel like they happened to you rather than with you?

Choice? That’s a joke. When you’re in that state, with your head pounding and the magnesium making everything feel fuzzy and disconnected, you don’t have the luxury of "choice." You have the illusion of it, maybe, if you’re lucky. But for me? Decisions didn't happen *with* me; they happened *to* me, like a freight train I couldn't get out of the way of.

There was no pause to say, "Genie, this is the risk, here’s why we’re doing this, what do you think?" It was just, "We’re doing this now." I felt like a spectator in my own labor. I’d try to speak, to ask a question, and the answer would be something like, "We need to do this for the baby," as if I wasn't already doing everything in my power to keep that baby safe. It’s an incredibly lonely, stripping feeling to be the one whose body is on the line, yet to have absolutely zero say in the path being taken. I wasn't an active participant; I was just the site of a procedure. By the time I realized what was happening, the decision had already been made by people who hadn't even looked me in the eye. That’s the part that still keeps me up at night.

During labour, what did you expect the midwife or doctor present to provide in terms of emotional support and personal attention — not just clinical care? And what did you actually experience from the staff who were with you?

I think, deep down, I wanted a bridge—someone to help me cross that gap between my clinical brain and my terrified heart. I expected them to be the anchor. I wanted them to acknowledge that I was scared, that being a single mom with two kids waiting at home made this birth feel like a high-stakes gamble. I didn't want them to fix my fear—I know they can't—but I wanted them to hold space for it. I expected a human touch, a soft voice amidst the alarms, someone to remind me that I was a person, not just a set of vitals.

The reality? It was a desert of emotional support. The staff were technically proficient, I suppose—they knew their stuff—but they were ghosts. They moved around me with a detached efficiency that felt colder than the equipment. There was no "How are you holding up, Genie?" or "I know this is a lot to carry right now." They didn't see the woman behind the chart; they saw the pre-eclampsia, the high-risk coding, and the mounting insurance complexities. If I cried or showed any sign of panic, they just checked another box. It made me feel like my feelings were an inconvenience, an obstacle to the process. I didn't just feel physically vulnerable; I felt completely invisible.
Was there a specific moment when you felt truly seen and supported — or a moment when you felt very alone? Can you describe it?

I remember this one moment, right as the magnesium was really hitting me. I felt like I was drifting, and the room was spinning. My head was throbbing, and I could hear the constant, rhythmic *beep-beep-beep* of the monitor. I looked at the nurse—I don’t even remember her name—and I just whispered, "I'm so scared I won't make it home to my kids." 

I wanted her to stop for a second, maybe just take my hand and tell me I was going to be okay. But she didn't stop. She didn't even look up from the screen she was adjusting. She just muttered something about "getting the pressure stabilized" and kept typing. 

In that moment, I felt a kind of isolation I can't even describe. I wasn't just alone in the room; I was alone in the entire universe. It was as if I’d already ceased to exist as a person, and I was just a medical complication being processed. I realized then that no one was looking out for me, the mother. They were only looking at the data points. I felt like I was screaming for someone to recognize me, but all they saw was a set of numbers that needed to be managed. I’ve never felt so small or so terrified in my entire life.

Before birth, what did you expect the immediate period after your baby was born to be like — skin-to-skin contact, who would be present, what would be done? And what actually happened in those first moments after delivery?

I had this picture in my head—I think every mother does. I wanted that golden hour. I wanted the room to quiet down, the lights to dim, and for them to place my baby on my chest so I could finally breathe. I imagined that moment of skin-to-skin contact would be the anchor, the reward for the months of terror and the fight for survival. I expected to see my baby’s face, to feel their warmth, and to finally feel the panic recede. I thought, *if I can just get to that moment, everything will be okay.*

But that isn't what happened at all. The birth was chaotic. The moment the baby was out, they were whisked away by a team that seemed to materialize from nowhere. There was no gentle hand-off, no calm introduction. It was all hurried movements and shouting. My baby was surrounded by a wall of people—I couldn't even see them, just hear the commotion. I was left alone on the table, still feeling the effects of the meds, shivering, and staring at the ceiling while the "clinical care" continued around me. 

I didn't hold my baby for what felt like forever. It wasn't a transition into motherhood; it was a continuation of the emergency. I was left behind, empty-handed and terrified, while the real focus of the room was elsewhere. It felt like I’d done the work, but I was denied the payoff. I just laid there, wondering if the distance between me and my baby was going to be the theme of the rest of my life.
Was there anything that happened — or didn't happen — in those first moments that still stays with you? What was it?

The thing that stays with me, the thing that replay in my mind when I can’t sleep, is the silence—my silence. Everyone else in that room was shouting out vitals, calling for equipment, and buzzing around my baby like a swarm of bees. But nobody spoke to me. 

I was lying there, completely spent, and I felt this crushing, physical weight of irrelevance. My baby was just a few feet away, but they might as well have been in another building. When they finally did bring the baby over, it was so hurried. The nurse held them up, did a quick, perfunctory "Here's your baby," and then, before I could even find the strength to lift my arms, they were taken away again for more monitoring. 

I didn't get to smell them. I didn't get to feel their skin against mine. I didn't get a "Good job, Mom, you made it." It was all clinical, all about the protocols. That disconnect—the way I was pushed aside while they focused on the data—it feels like a theft. I was present for the delivery, but I wasn't allowed to be present for the birth. It’s like they were so busy saving my life that they forgot to let me live it. That feeling of being disposable? It hasn't left me.
Before giving birth, what did you expect your physical recovery to look and feel like in the weeks after? And how did the reality of your physical recovery compare to those expectations?

I expected the recovery to be exhausting, of course—I’m a mom, I know what a newborn does to your sleep schedule—but I thought it would be a linear process. I figured I’d be sore, maybe a little overwhelmed, but that I’d be able to focus on healing my body and bonding with the baby. I thought, "I’ve survived the worst part," and I expected to feel the weight of that fear finally lifting off my chest as the days passed.

The reality hit like a brick wall. My physical recovery wasn't just about healing from the birth; it felt like I was recovering from a trauma I hadn't been given the space to process. My body was still carrying that "high-risk" label even after leaving the hospital—I was constantly checking my blood pressure, terrified that the pre-eclampsia was going to flare up again. Every headache, every bit of dizziness sent me spiraling, thinking I was failing my kids because I couldn't be the mother they needed while I was so physically fragile.

I didn't account for the emotional wreckage. I spent those weeks in a fog, constantly replaying the birth, feeling angry about how I was sidelined and terrified that I was broken beyond repair. My recovery hasn't just been physical; it’s been a fight to reclaim my sense of self from the sterile, cold hands of that hospital room.

Before your baby arrived, what did you expect feeding — breastfeeding or formula feeding — to be like in terms of ease, support, and how you'd feel about it? And what was the reality of feeding in the early weeks?

I honestly thought feeding would be the one place where I could just be a mother, no charts, no monitors, no "high-risk" protocols. I imagined it would be instinctive—that maybe, just maybe, after everything the hospital put me through, this would be the quiet, peaceful time where I could finally bond with my baby. I expected it to be a bit of a learning curve, sure, but I didn't expect to feel so completely unmoored.

The reality was a complete nightmare. My body felt like it was still healing from the trauma of the delivery, and with the residual stress and the exhaustion of managing the other kids on my own, my milk just didn't come in the way the brochures promised. I felt like I was failing at the only thing that was supposed to be simple. Every time I reached for the formula, I felt this wave of shame, like I was confirming that I just wasn't enough. I needed someone to sit with me, to validate that it was okay to struggle, but I felt so isolated in my little apartment. 

There was no support. I was just another tired, worried mother, feeling guilty for not being able to provide that "perfect" experience. I spent those early weeks crying over a bottle, terrified that the baby could sense my anxiety. It wasn't the bonding experience I dreamed of; it was just one more battle I felt like I was losing.
Before your baby arrived, what did you expect your emotional and mental health to be like in the postpartum period? And what was your actual emotional experience in the weeks and months after birth?

I honestly expected that the moment I walked out of that hospital, I’d be hit with this overwhelming rush of "new mom" joy. I knew it would be tough with two other kids, but I imagined a sense of relief—a, "We survived, we’re home, now we can breathe." I thought I’d just settle into a new, albeit chaotic, routine. I expected to feel like *me* again, just with a little more love to go around.

But the reality? It’s like I never actually left the trauma of the birth room. I’m constantly looking over my shoulder, waiting for the other shoe to drop. My mental health feels like a frayed wire. I’m so hyper-vigilant about my blood pressure, my headache, the kids' schedules—I’m in a perpetual state of "fight or flight." It’s not just the hormones; it’s the ghost of that birth experience. I feel angry, mostly. I’m angry that no one told me it was okay to be scared, or that the way they treated me made me feel so small. 

I’m struggling to feel connected to the baby because I’m so exhausted from just keeping my head above water. Every time I look at them, I’m reminded of how close I came to not being there. It’s like I’m grieving a birth I never got to have, all while trying to keep my family from falling apart. It’s not "postpartum blues"; it’s a deep, cold, lonely kind of survival mode that no one prepared me for.
Before becoming a parent, what did you expect your sense of identity to feel like after your baby arrived? And how has your sense of who you are actually shifted since then?

Before this, I thought motherhood was just a new layer I’d add to my existing self. I was Genie—the woman who worked in clinics, the one who understood the science, the one who handled everything with a steady hand. I thought I’d be this grounded, capable woman who just happened to have an extra person to love. I honestly thought that identity was something solid, something I could protect.

But the reality? It’s like my identity was dismantled in that delivery room and never quite put back together. I feel like a shadow of the person I was. That medicalized, cold experience stripped away my belief that I could advocate for myself or control my own path. I’m not just a mom; I’m a survivor of a system that didn't see me as a person, and that changes you.

I don’t feel like that professional, capable woman anymore. I feel like a fragile, anxious mother who is constantly waiting for the next crisis. It’s hard to look in the mirror and see "Genie." All I see is the trauma, the fear for my kids’ future, and the weight of being alone in this. I’ve lost that sense of who I was before the monitor started beeping, and I have no idea how to find her again. It feels like I’ve been rebuilt, but I’m missing some of the essential pieces.
Were there parts of your previous identity — professional, social, personal — that felt harder to hold onto than you expected?

It’s the professional part that cuts the deepest. I spent years in clinical settings, feeling like I understood the language, the hierarchy, the "why" behind the protocols. I took pride in that. I thought that knowledge would be my armor. But when I was in that delivery room, it didn't protect me; it made me realize exactly what was being done to me, and that just amplified the helplessness. I couldn't "out-think" the situation, and the fact that my medical background was completely ignored by the staff made me feel like my entire professional life was a lie. How could I trust my own judgment again when they treated my expertise as if it were nonexistent?

Socially, I feel like I’m wearing a mask. People ask how I’m doing, and I say "I’m fine," because how do you explain to a friend that you’re grieving a version of yourself that died on an operating table? I can’t go back to being the "reliable one" who handles everything with a smile, because I’m running on fumes and constant terror. I’ve retreated. I don't go out. I feel like if I step outside, the whole fragile structure of my life—the kids, the bills, my mental stability—will just collapse. I’ve lost my footing, and I don't know how to reach for the person I used to be.
After birth, what did you expect the postnatal check-ups and follow-up care from your midwife or GP to provide? And what did that post-birth care actually look like in practice?

I expected the follow-ups to be a place of reconciliation—a time where someone would finally sit down and walk me through what happened, acknowledge the trauma, and help me bridge the gap between "medical patient" and "new mother." I thought they would ask, "How are you *really* doing?" and actually want to hear the answer. I wanted them to validate that my fear was a normal response to being managed like a broken machine rather than a person.

The reality was clinical, brief, and painfully empty. My appointments were basically just blood pressure checks and a quick, "Everything looks stable, see you in six weeks." They were fixated on the numbers—the pre-eclampsia markers—and ignored the woman sitting right in front of them who was clearly fraying at the edges. When I tried to bring up the birth or how I was struggling, the conversation was quickly steered back to medication or my physical vitals. 

It felt like they were just checking to make sure I wasn't going to have a stroke, not checking to see if I was actually *alive*. They provided zero emotional follow-up, no acknowledgement of the birth, and absolutely no sense of partnership. I left those appointments feeling even more isolated than when I walked in. It was like I was just a case file that needed to be closed out, and once the numbers looked "fine," they were done with me.
Were there concerns you raised at postnatal appointments that you felt were not taken seriously or properly followed up?

I tried, in my own way, to bring it up. At one of my first follow-ups, I mentioned the nightmares—you know, the ones where I’m back on that table, the alarms are screaming, and I can’t move. I told the provider that the magnesium fog hadn't really lifted, that I was feeling this crushing, lingering panic whenever I heard a sudden noise or a beep. I wasn't asking for a cure; I was asking for someone to just *acknowledge* that what I went through had broken something in me.

The response was so dismissive it was physical, like a slap. They looked at their watch, glanced at my blood pressure reading, and said something about "the stress of a newborn" and "it’s normal to feel overwhelmed." They didn't even look at me. They reduced my terror to a generic "new mom stress" checkbox. It felt like they were actively working to keep the conversation clinical so they wouldn't have to deal with the messy reality of what they—or the system they represent—had done to me. I felt silenced. I realized then that if I wanted real help, I wasn't going to get it from the people who presided over that birth. I was on my own, just like I was in that hospital room, left to figure out how to put myself back together while keeping my kids safe.
If you returned to work after your baby was born, what did you expect that transition to be like — practically and emotionally? And what did returning to work actually feel like?

Returning to work was supposed to be a return to normalcy. I thought that by re-entering a familiar professional environment, I’d reclaim some of the "Genie" that existed before the pregnancy. I expected to feel competent again, to have conversations that weren't about infant feeding or my blood pressure, and to find structure in the routine. I figured the transition would be logistically hard—juggling childcare, the lingering physical exhaustion, and the tight budget—but I thought my work would be a sanctuary.

The reality? It was jarring, bordering on dissociative. Being back in a health setting felt like walking into a crime scene every single day. I’d walk past the unit where I gave birth, and I’d feel the floor drop out from under me. I’d see a patient monitor and my heart would start racing, my chest tightening just like it did on that bed. It wasn't a sanctuary; it was a constant, visual trigger of my own trauma. 

Emotionally, I felt like an imposter. I was still struggling with such intense anxiety and that deep, hollow sense of being "used" by the system that I couldn't focus. I’d be talking to a patient, and suddenly I’d be transported back to my own birth, feeling that same helplessness. It’s been an exhausting, lonely experience of trying to be the professional I once was, all while hiding the fact that I’m still internally screaming. I’m just waiting for the day I don’t feel like I’m about to fall apart in the middle of a shift.
Were there practical aspects of returning to work — feeding, childcare, your own health — that were harder to manage than you expected?

It’s the sheer, crushing weight of holding it all together that surprised me. I thought I could compartmentalize—work here, home there, kids everywhere—but the reality is that I’m constantly leaking stress from one area into the other. 

The physical recovery from pre-eclampsia means I’m often dealing with this low-grade, persistent exhaustion and the constant, nagging fear that my body is going to give out again. But I can't show that at work. I have to be the one who’s "on." Trying to manage the logistics—the childcare drop-offs, the constant worry about the baby’s health while I’m on a shift—it’s like trying to keep a house of cards from blowing over in a gale. 

And feeding? Forget about it. Trying to find time, space, and peace to pump in a hospital setting where I’m already triggered by the alarms and the pace... it’s impossible. It feels like every minute I spend at work, I’m failing at home, and every minute I’m at home, I’m failing at work. I’m physically and mentally spread so thin that I’m basically transparent. No one at work sees the shaking hands or the panic in my chest when a monitor alarms; they just see "Genie." I’ve had to bury my own needs so deep just to function that I don’t even recognize myself in the mirror anymore.

After your baby arrived, what did you expect your relationship with your partner to be like in terms of sharing caregiving, managing the relationship, and supporting each other? And what has that relationship dynamic actually looked like?

I expected a team. I really, truly believed that when things got tough—and I knew they would—we would be in the trenches together. I pictured us trading off feedings, maybe whispering about the kids' futures while they slept, and just having that solid, quiet companionship that comes from shared struggle. I thought we would be two people holding up the same heavy, beautiful roof.

But the reality? It’s been more like two people drifting on separate rafts in a storm. My husband… he just isn't here in the way I need. It’s not that he’s *trying* to be absent, but he doesn't understand the depth of the fear that still grips me. He’s back to "normal," worried about the bills, the job, the day-to-day. When I try to tell him about the trauma, or why I freeze up when I hear a medical alarm, he looks at me like I’m speaking a language he doesn't know. He thinks the "danger" is over because the baby is here. He doesn’t see that I’m still standing in that delivery room, paralyzed.

The emotional labor is entirely on me. I’m the one managing the household, the kids, the budget, *and* the PTSD. I feel like a single mother even when he’s sitting right there on the couch. It’s the loneliest thing I’ve ever felt.

Looking across your entire maternity journey — from trying to conceive through to where you are now — what did you expect the overall experience of maternity care to deliver? And reflecting on the whole journey, what was the most significant gap between what you expected and what you received?

I expected the system to be a safety net—one that would catch me precisely because I was "high-risk." I thought my medical background would make me an equal, a collaborator who could navigate the complexity of pre-eclampsia with dignity. I expected that even if the birth was scary, the people caring for me would honor my autonomy and see me as a human being worthy of care, not just a set of data points to be managed before the next shift began.

The most significant gap, and the one that still burns, is the total erasure of my humanity. I expected a partnership, but I received a cold, hurried, and dismissive process that felt more like being processed through a machine than being helped to give birth. That gap between being "cared for" and being "managed" is where I’ve lost myself. 

They kept me alive, yes—and I’m grateful for that, for my kids' sake—but they didn't protect my spirit. I walked in thinking I was an intelligent, capable professional, and I walked out feeling like I was just a liability that they were relieved to sign off on. They focused so much on the physiology of my pregnancy that they completely neglected the psychology of my existence. I’m still trying to fill that hollow space where my agency used to be.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

You know, there’s one more thing that feels important. People talk about "maternal health" as if it’s just about survival rates and healthy babies—like, if you walk out of the hospital with a breathing infant and a pulse, you’ve "won." But that’s a dangerous oversimplification. 

It feels like there’s this massive, silent assumption that if you have a medical background—if you’re a nurse, or you’ve worked in the clinics—you somehow don’t need the same emotional care as everyone else. Like I’m expected to handle the trauma because I "understand the science." But knowing how a magnesium drip works doesn’t mean I don’t feel the terror of being tethered to it. Knowing the statistics on mortality doesn't make me any less afraid of not coming home to my kids. 

If I could say anything to the people who run these systems, it’s this: stop looking at the files and start looking at the mothers. We aren’t just the "complicated cases" or the "high-risk" statistics. We are women who are terrified, who are grieving, and who are trying to navigate a system that feels designed to strip us of our voices. I just wish someone—anyone—had taken the time to ask me how I was *feeling* instead of just asking what my blood pressure was. A little bit of humanity could have saved me from so much of this darkness.
